Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Lets analyze the process step by step to determine the constraints The process starts with Receive Order RO Then typically we check availability CA So RO should occur before CA

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: the constraint Always Before is defined as If the first activity occurs then the second activity should have been executed previously This means if the first activity occurs then the second must have already occurred at some point before We can set RO must be always before CA Actually in the process RO must occur before CA However note that the constraint is always_before x y means if x occurs then y must have occurred before So for CA RO if CA occurs then RO must have occurred before That is true Alternatively we can set RO CA in always_after if RO occurs then CA must occur after

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Lets list the natural order or dependencies in the process RO CA RI PI QC PO GSL D IC RP However note that not all activities are necessarily in a strict linear order for every case But from the description it seems the process is sequential The problem does not mention parallelism so we assume a sequential process with the above order

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: But note IC and RP might occur after D but not necessarily immediately after And IC and RP might be independent The description says Dispatch D The package is sent out to the carrier Invoice Customer IC An invoice is generated and sent to the customer Record Payment RP The payment record is updated in the system once the customer pays Typically after dispatch we invoice and then record payment But note that RP occurs when the customer pays which might be after IC However the process might allow IC and RP to occur in any order The description doesnt specify Given the sequential nature of the other activities lets assume the entire process is sequential in the order given

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We need to define the following keys equivalence always_before always_after never_together activ_freq directly_follows Lets consider each constraint a Equivalence Do any two activities always occur together with the same frequency In this process I dont see any two activities that are strictly equivalent Well leave equivalence as an empty set b Always Before if PI occurs then RI must have occurred before Lets define for the sequence RO CA RI PI QC PO GSL D IC RP We can set CA RO RI CA PI RI QC PI PO QC GSL PO D GSL IC D RP IC Well set the pairs for consecutive activities in the assumed order c Always After This is the reverse of always_before So for the sequence we can set RO CA CA RI RI PI PI QC QC PO PO GSL GSL D D IC IC RP

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: d Never Together Are there any two activities that cannot occur in the same case In this process all activities are part of the same order so they should occur together We are not told of any activities that are mutually exclusive So we leave never_together as an empty set e Activity Occurrences From the process each activity is expected to occur exactly once in a normal case The description does not mention any loops So we assume each activity occurs exactly once The description says every order goes through these activities So we set RO 1 CA 1 RI 1 PI 1 QC 1 PO 1 GSL 1 D 1 IC 1 RP 1 We are modeling the process for a successfully completed order So we assume all activities occur

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: f Directly Follows This enforces that if the first activity occurs then the second must occur immediately after In the sequential process we have RO CA CA RI RI PI PI QC QC PO PO GSL GSL D D IC IC RP So we set the directly_follows for consecutive activities in the sequence

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Lets construct the dictionary accordingly However note that the constraints we set might be redundant However note that the process might allow some flexibility For example the invoice and payment recording might occur in parallel or in any order after dispatch But the problem does not specify so we stick to the linear order Lets code accordingly
